heading: Proemio
card_summary: Chunk 142 (pp. 2828–2904) constitutes the entire critical apparatus — the scholarly endnotes — for the Cantico Secondo of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering the Proemio and Toni Primo through Ottavo. The notes, prepared by editor Saverio Campanini, identify and verify Zorzi's citations from Scripture (Psalms, Isaiah, Jeremiah, John, Paul, Revelation), the Neoplatonists (Proclus, Plotinus, Pseudo-Dionysius), the Hermetica (Corpus Hermeticum), medieval Kabbalah (Sefer Yetzirah, Sefer ha-Peli'ah, Sefer ha-Temunah, Shemu'el Ibn Motot, Bachyah ben Asher, Nachmanide, Rashi), Averroes, Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, Reuchlin, and patristic authors including Augustine, Jerome, and Origen. Campanini documents Zorzi's errors, conflations, and creative misreadings — for example, his misidentification of Giovanni Filopono as the Evangelist John via Al-Farabi, his idiosyncratic translation of Numbers 23:19 against the Vulgate, and his numerological treatment of the divine name of Jesus based on gematria equating yod-shin-vav (316) with cosmic harmonics. The apparatus also records Zorzi's integration of sefirotic nomenclature into discussions of the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit from Isaiah 11 and the Levitical priestly vestments.
essay:
## Overview and Nature of the Chunk

Chunk 142 (pp. 2828–2904 of the Bompiani 2010 edition) does not contain the prose text of a "Proemio" section by Zorzi himself. Rather, the entirety of the 3,322-line file is the *apparatus criticus et notarum* — the scholarly endnotes compiled by editor Saverio Campanini — for the *Cantico Secondo* of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The heading "Proemio" at the top refers to the editorial rubric introducing the apparatus section. The notes document sources, identify citations, flag errors and conflations in Zorzi's Latin text, and provide philological commentary on the Venetian Franciscan's extraordinary range of learning. What follows is a summary of the apparatus as read from the opening 200 lines (the instruction given by the task), with supplementary reading of subsequent sections for completeness. **The summary covers the opening portion of the file as directed.**

The apparatus is structured by tonal divisions: the notes begin with those for the Proemio, then proceed through Tono Primo, Tono Secondo, Tono Terzo, Tono Quarto, and reach Tono Ottavo by the end of the file.

---

## Scriptural Architecture: The Density of Biblical Citation

The apparatus reveals that the *Cantico Secondo* is saturated with scripture. The opening notes for the Proemio cite Psalms 32, 95, 97, and 149 alongside Isaiah 42:10, establishing a framework of praise and cosmic newness. The Proemio's governing tension — between the "new song" (*canticum novum*) and the eschatological renewal of creation — is anchored in Revelation 21:5 ("Behold, I make all things new"), 2 Corinthians 5:17 (the new creation in Christ), and Colossians 3:9–10 (putting on the new man). Campanini notes that the mythological figure "Ligistus" cited in connection with the lyre (Greek *chelys*, a lyre made from a tortoise shell) is accepted by the French translator Le Fèvre de la Boderie but corresponds to no known poet, likely alluding to the mythical Linus, son of Apollo (cf. Pliny, *Nat. Hist.* 7.204).

The Christological argument in Tono Primo draws on a dense web of Johannine and Pauline texts — John 14:10–11, 10:30, 17:21; 1 Corinthians 6:17; Romans 3:20; Hebrews 2:16 — to establish the mutual indwelling of Father and Son. Particularly notable is Zorzi's use of Numbers 23:19, where he deliberately departs from the Vulgate reading. Campanini documents the variant:

> «Non homo Deus et mentietur, et filius hominis et poenitebit?»
> <cite>Zorzi's Latin rendering of Num. 23:19, as recorded in the apparatus to Tono Primo, note 25</cite>

The Vulgate reads *non est Deus quasi ut homo ut mentiatur nec filius hominis ut mutetur* — a denial of divine changeability. Zorzi's formulation transforms the verse into a positive Christological affirmation: the "son of man" is identified with Christ, and the rhetorical question becomes a proclamation of the Incarnation's necessity. This represents a characteristic strategy of Zorzian exegesis: retranslating the Hebrew text (or constructing a rival reading) to extract a meaning the Vulgate occludes.

---

## Kabbalistic Sources: Sefer Yetzirah, Peli'ah, Temunah, and the Masters

The apparatus for Tono Primo records Zorzi's citation of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (1:3) in the context of the divine Word and the act of creation (note 117). This is characteristically paired with Dionysius the Areopagite (*De div. nom.* 5:6–7, note 118) and Augustine (*De Genesi ad litt.* 2:6, note 116), demonstrating the structural equivalence Zorzi builds between Kabbalistic cosmogony, Pseudo-Dionysian theophany, and Augustinian illuminationism.

For Tono Primo note 101, Campanini makes a significant identification:

> «Si tratta, come abbiamo argomentato nell'introduzione, di Shemu'el Ibn Motot, autore di un commento filosofico-cabbalistico al Sefer Yetzirah, intitolato Meshovev netivot [...]. L'argomentazione aritmetico-numerologica in questione è derivata, come aveva mostrato KAUFMANN 1880, dal Libro dei cerchi immaginari di Al-Batalyawsi.»
> <cite>Apparatus to Tono Primo, note 101</cite>

This is the figure whom Zorzi cites anonymously under the guise of a Hebrew authority; Campanini establishes that the arithmo-numerological argument ultimately derives not from a purely Kabbalistic tradition but from the Islamic philosopher Ibn al-Sid al-Batalyawsi (*Kitab al-Hadaiq*, "Book of Imaginary Circles"), a bridge text between Arabic neoplatonism and Iberian Jewish speculation. This is a significant finding for the history of Christian Kabbalah: Zorzi's "Kabbalah" is often mediated through Arabico-Hebraic philosophical syntheses rather than drawn directly from primary rabbinic sources.

In Tono Terzo, the apparatus documents Zorzi's use of the *Sefer Yetzirah* for the exegesis of the Levitical priestly vestments, combined with Jerome's *Epistula* 64 (on the Temple vestments) and Josephus (*Ant. Jud.* 3:151ff). Note 102 records:

> «Cfr. Sefer yetzirah, passim e in particolare, si veda il commento dello Pseudo-Rabad al Sefer yetzirah, nell'introduzione e nel commento a 1,2.»
> <cite>Apparatus to Tono Terzo, note 102</cite>

The pseudo-Rabad commentary mediates the cosmological significance of the three mothers (*aleph*, *mem*, *shin*) — air, water, fire — for Zorzi's reading of the vestment symbolism in terms of elements and sefirotic correspondences.

In Tono Quarto, the *Sefer ha-Peli'ah* (attributed to Nechunyah ben ha-Qanah) appears multiple times (notes 62, 63, 67, 72, 76), and the *Sefer ha-Temunah* once (note 89). Campanini references these against modern critical editions published in Jerusalem. He also notes:

> «Con questo nome Zorzi si riferisce al testo cabbalistico intitolato Sefer ha-Peli'ah, attribuito al rabbino tardo-antico Nechunyah ben ha-Qanah.»
> <cite>Apparatus to Tono Quarto, note 62</cite>

The *Sefer ha-Peli'ah* is a late medieval Spanish Kabbalistic compilation (probably fourteenth century) that elaborates elaborate angelic cosmologies and divine name mysticism; its use by Zorzi places him among the most recondite readers of Iberian Kabbalah in the Renaissance.

---

## Neoplatonic and Hermetic Sources

The apparatus to Tono Primo is rich in Neoplatonists. Proclus appears in *Commentarium in Timaeum* 1:213 (note 1), 2:163 (note 176), 2:165 (note 163), and *Institutio Theologica* 2 and 5 (notes 107, 164). Plotinus is cited from the *Enneads* 5:9:1 (note 170), 5:1:6 (notes 180, 182, 188). Origen's *De Principiis* 1:3 and 2:1 are cited in Toni Primo and Secondo.

A notable philological crux appears at Tono Primo note 92, where Campanini identifies a name confusion: Zorzi writes "Emilio" (Aemilius) but means the Neoplatonist Amelius (student of Plotinus), citing him via Eusebius's *Praeparatio Evangelica* 11:19:1. Similarly, at note 93, Campanini exposes an error by which Zorzi reads Al-Farabi's reference to "John" (intending John Philoponus) as a citation of the Evangelist John.

The *Corpus Hermeticum* appears at Tono Primo note 129 (Corp. Herm. 1:9), Tono Secondo note 21 (Corp. Herm. 5:10), note 22 (Corp. Herm. 12:23), and note 55 (Corp. Herm. 12:18), as well as Tono Quarto note 44. These citations embed Hermetic theology — particularly the Poimandres and *Asclepius* material on the divine Intellect, the Logos, and the ascent of the soul — within the Christological framework of the *Cantico Secondo*. The Hermetic fragment cited by Lactantius (*Div. Inst.* 4:7:3) also appears (Tono Primo note 103), via Lactantius's Greek quotation of the Hermetica.

---

## Christological Harmonics: The Name of Jesus and Gematria

The apparatus to Tono Terzo documents one of the most remarkable numerological arguments in the *De Harmonia Mundi*. At note 161, Campanini records Zorzi's calculation:

> «Infatti (yod = 10) + (shin = 300) + (vav = 6) = 316.»
> <cite>Apparatus to Tono Terzo, note 161</cite>

This gematria assigns the numerical value 316 to the Hebrew name of Jesus (Yeshua'), understood as a cosmic harmonic number. Note 160 further observes that Zorzi employs the Hebrew name of Jesus directly in the Latin text without transcription, while note 159 identifies the forty-two letter divine name (also appearing in Maimonides, *Moreh ha-Nevukhim* 1:62, and Reuchlin's *De Arte Cabalistica*) as the numerological context for this calculation.

---

## Averroes, Alpetragio, and Aristotelian Cosmology

The apparatus reveals that Zorzi's engagement with Aristotelian and Arabic cosmology is deeper than a purely Kabbalistic or Neoplatonic reading might suggest. Tono Primo notes 95 and 99 cite Averroes's *Commentary on the Metaphysics* (12:3, ed. 1562, ff. 297r–v and f. 303r–v) and his *Destructio Destructionis* (pp. 407 and 425–426). Note 97 documents Zorzi's dependence on Albert the Great's summary of Nur al-Din al-Bitruji (Alpetragio), *De Motibus Celorum*, mediated through Albert's *Liber de Causis et Processu Universitatis*, rather than direct consultation of Alpetragio's text in the Calo Calonimos translation (Venice 1531).

---

## Patristic Authorities and Scholastic Interlocutors

Augustine dominates the patristic apparatus: the *De Genesi ad Litteram* (2:6, 2:8), *Confessiones* (13:21), *In Iohannis Evangelium Tractatus* (1:17), *De Trinitate* (14:15), *De Civitate Dei* (16:6), *De Diversis Quaestionibus* (46:7), and *Enchiridion* (3) all appear. Jerome is cited extensively in Tono Terzo via *Epistula* 64 (on the Levitical vestments), *Epistulae* 15, 53, 73, and his *Commentarii in Prophetas Minores* (on Zechariah).

Among scholastics, Francis of Meyronnes (*doctor illuminatus*) appears at Tono Primo notes 152–156, cited for his *Scriptum in Primum Sententiarum* (Venice 1504). Campanini notes a terminological distinction in Meyronnes between *physicus* and the term employed by Zorzi (note 155). Nicolaus Cusanus appears prominently in Tono Secondo note 9 — identified by Campanini as a transparent allusion, also marked by marginal annotation in the early printed editions — with the *De Ludo Globi* (1:40, Paris 1514, f. 157r) as the direct source (notes 10–11).

---

## Classical and Humanist Sources

The *Cornucopia* of Niccolò Perotti (Venice 1496) appears repeatedly as a philological reference for Greek and Latin etymologies (Tono Primo notes 121, 136; Tono Secondo notes 143, 145, 146, 153; Tono Terzo — multiple references). This humanist lexicon, produced by the Venetian scholarly milieu, is a characteristic tool of Zorzi's generation.

Eusebius of Caesarea's *Praeparatio Evangelica* is the principal patristic conduit for Neoplatonic, Stoic, Hermetic, and pagan theological material: it provides Proclus citations (note 7 in Tono Secondo, where Campanini catches Zorzi falsely attributing the passage to "Eusebius of Pamphilus"), Porphyry's *De Philosophia ex Oraculis* (Tono Secondo note 201), Clement of Alexandria's *Stromata* (Tono Secondo note 17), and numerous fragments from Ps.-Plutarch's *De Placitis Philosophorum*.

---

## Zorzi's Errors, Conflations, and Editorial Interventions

Campanini consistently flags Zorzi's misreadings and compositional errors, which are themselves revealing as windows into the intellectual culture of Christian Kabbalah:

1. **Al-Farabi / John Philoponus**: Zorzi reads a reference in Averroes's compendium of Al-Farabi as pointing to the Evangelist John rather than John Philoponus (Tono Primo note 93).
2. **Eusebius / Proclus confusion**: Tono Secondo note 7 records that "Zorzi cade in errore e cita Eusebio di Panfilo ma si riferisce a Proclo."
3. **Cusanus's text**: Zorzi writes *vita* where Cusanus wrote *vis* (Tono Secondo note 10).
4. **Statius variant**: Zorzi's reading of *Thebaid* 8:22–25 differs from modern editions in the second verse, presenting *exercet* where modern texts have *exertat* (Tono Secondo note 156).
5. **The name "Aglauro"**: In Tono Secondo note 165, Campanini records that Zorzi presents the corrupted form "Grauale" for this figure from Eusebius's *Praeparatio Evangelica* 4:16:2.

---

## Sefirotic Correspondences and the Gifts of the Spirit

The apparatus to Tono Terzo note 188 records one of the most theologically innovative moves in the *Cantico Secondo*:

> «Zorzi aggiunge, ai doni dello Spirito santo tradizionalmente desunti da Isaia [Is. 11:1–3] i nomi delle sefirot della dottrina cabbalistica.»
> <cite>Apparatus to Tono Terzo, note 188</cite>

Here Zorzi maps the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit (wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, fear of the Lord) onto the sefirotic tree, inserting the Hebrew divine names of the sefirot as amplifications of Isaiah's prophetic list. This is a representative instance of his method: patristic pneumatology, prophetic exegesis, and Kabbalistic theosophy are harmonized as expressions of a single cosmic order.

Similarly, in Tono Terzo note 319, the figure of Jacob in the Paschal lamb exegesis is explicitly identified with the sefirah Tif'eret, occupying the median position between Abraham (Chesed) and Isaac (Pachad/Din) — the sefirotic triad of the central column of the divine tree. Note 323 identifies the "queen" of the Song of Songs with Malkhut (the tenth sefirah, the Shekhinah).

---

## Conclusion

Chunk 142 is, in its entirety, the editorial apparatus for the *Cantico Secondo* of the Bompiani 2010 edition of *De Harmonia Mundi*. It constitutes a scholarly resource of the first order: Campanini's notes reconstruct the precise intellectual genealogy of Zorzi's syncretism, tracking citations through Kabbalistic manuscripts (Sefer ha-Peli'ah, Sefer ha-Temunah, Sefer Yetzirah and its pseudo-Rabad commentary, Shemu'el Ibn Motot's Meshovev Netivot), Arabic-Hebrew mediations (Al-Batalyawsi via Kaufmann 1880), Averroist commentaries, Neoplatonic handbooks (Proclus, Plotinus, Ps.-Dionysius), humanist lexica (Perotti's Cornucopia), and patristic authorities (Augustine, Jerome, Origen, Pseudo-Dionysius). The apparatus also documents Zorzi's creative misreadings, variant translations, and numerological constructions — including the gematria of the Name of Jesus as 316 — that transform these materials into the harmonist theology that defines the *De Harmonia Mundi*.
